title: Manipulation of diverse quantum correlations based on a hybrid optomagnomechanical system
authors: Xiaomin Liu, Rongguo Yang, Jing Zhang, Tiancai Zhang
abstract: Flexible manipulation of quantum correlation resources enables the implementation of diverse quantum tasks based on hybrid quantum networks, where atom-magnon and optomagnonic entanglements and steerings play important roles. In this work, we propose an effective scheme to generate and manipulate quantum entanglements and steerings based on a hybrid optomagnomechanical system, which is composed of a polarizer, an optical cavity with YIG bridge as one end, and an atomic ensemble in it. According to the results of the parameter dependence of various quantum correlations, we can selectively generate bipartite and genuine tripartite entanglements and deterministically manipulate the concrete situation of bipartite, multipartite steerings, and collective pentapartite steering, by adjusting the polarization direction of the driving laser and the Tavis-Cummings coupling strength. Our all-optical controlled scheme is flexible, convenient, compact, and experimentally feasible, because multiple coupling channels can be tuned simultaneously. This work provides a new perspective for implementing specialized quantum tasks, such as hierarchical ultra-secure multi-user quantum communications.
summary: This paper proposes a scheme to control various quantum correlations in a hybrid optomagnomechanical system using only light polarization. By adjusting the driving laser's polarization angle, the researchers can switch between different types of entanglement and quantum steering, including complex pentapartite steering. The system integrates optical, magnonic, and atomic modes, providing a flexible way to route quantum information. This all-optical approach is experimentally feasible and could be used for advanced quantum communication tasks.
main_problem: The need for flexible and efficient manipulation of diverse quantum correlations (entanglement and steering) to implement various tasks in hybrid quantum networks without physical reconfiguration of hardware.
main_result: The authors demonstrate an all-optical scheme to selectively generate bipartite and tripartite entanglements and deterministically manipulate various types of quantum steering, including collective pentapartite steering, by adjusting laser polarization and coupling strength.
method: The study uses a linearized Quantum Langevin Equation approach and solves the Lyapunov equation to obtain the system's covariance matrix under a low-excitation, bosonic approximation.
model_or_system: A hybrid optomagnomechanical system consisting of an optical cavity with a YIG microbridge, an atomic ensemble, and a polarizer, involving interactions between optical modes, magnon, phonon, and atom modes.
key_observables: Logarithmic negativity (bipartite entanglement), minimum residual contangle (tripartite entanglement), and various steerability coefficients (one-way, two-way, and pentapartite steering).
important_parameters: Polarization angle (theta), Tavis-Cummings coupling strength (g_ac2), magnomechanical coupling (g_m), optomechanical coupling (g_c), and temperature (T < 4 K).
main_assumptions: Low-excitation approximation for the atomic ensemble, bosonic approximation for atomic polarization, and a Markovian approximation for noise terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the system schematic and interaction; Figure 6 illustrates tripartite steering regimes (one-way, two-way, no-way) as a function of coupling and polarization; Figure 7 shows quadripartite steering results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem, describes the hybrid optomagnomechanical system and its Hamiltonian, details the mathematical method for calculating correlations, presents results on entanglement and steering manipulation, and concludes with potential applications.
why_interesting: It presents a versatile method for all-optical control of complex, multi-party quantum correlations in a hybrid platform, which is highly relevant for developing scalable quantum networks and secure multi-user communication.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The hybrid optomagnomechanical system. (a) Schematic diagram; (b) Interaction relation (blue/red circles denote blue/red-detuned driving); (c) Frequency relation.

Figure 1(a) depicts a hybrid optomagnomechanical system driven by linearly polarized light, whose polar- ization direction is tunable via rotating a polarizer. The system consists of an optical cavity formed by a mir- ror and a YIG microbridge [6, 21] (its magnon (phonon) mode m (b) has frequency ωm (ωb) and decay rate κm (γb)) and an ensemble of Na two-level atoms (with fre- quency ωa and decay rate γa). The cavity mode can be decomposed into horizontal (c1) and vertical (c2) polar- ized modes at frequency ωc, with decay rates κc1, κc2, respectively. The interaction between the atomic ensem- ble and the cavity mode is a typical TC coupling. No- tably, only the c2 mode can effectively...

FIG. 3. Results of bipartite entanglements versus coupling and decoherence factors: (Eam, Ec1m, Ec2m) versus the effec- tive magnomechanical coupling strength Gm (a), the effective optomechanical coupling strength Gc (b), the magnon decay rate κm (f), the phonon decay rate γb (g), and the temper- ature T (h); Eam (c), Ec1m (d), Ec2m (e) versus the optical decay rates κc1 and κc2. All parameters remain the same as those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 2. Results of bipartite entanglements versus detunings: the atom-magnon entanglement Eam (a), the optomagnonic entanglements Ec1m (b) and Ec2m (c) versus ∆a and ∆m, and (Eam, Ec1m, Ec2m) versus ∆c (d). Except for the varied parameters, we set ωm/2π = 10 GHz, ωb/2π = 40 MHz, γa/2π = 1 MHz, κc1/2π = 3 MHz, κc2/2π = 1 MHz, κm/2π = 1 MHz, γb/2π = 100 Hz, gac2/2π = 3 MHz, θ = π/4, T = 10 mK, Gc/2π = 10 MHz, Gm/2π = 2 MHz, and ∆a = −∆m = ∆c = ωb.

FIG. 4. Results of bipartite and genuine tripartite entangle- ments versus the TC coupling strength gac2 and the polar- ization direction θ: Eam (a), Ec1m (b), Ec2m (c), Rac2m (d), Rac1m (e), and Rc1c2m (f). The results of bipartite and gen- uine tripartite entanglements are summarized in (g) and (h), respectively. We set Gm/2π = 3 MHz, and all other parame- ters remain the same as those in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Results of bipartite steerings versus gac2 and θ: Sm→a (a), Sm→c1 (b), Sm→c2 (c), and Sm→b (d). All results are summarized in (e). The blank area represents the unstable regime. All parameters remain the same as those in Fig. 4.

FIG. 7. Results of quadripartite steerings versus gac2 and θ: Smb→c1c2 (a), Sc1c2→mb (b), Sam→c1c2 (d), Sc1c2→am (e). The results involving mb and am are summarized in (c) and (f), respectively. The blank area represents the unstable regime. All parameters remain the same as those in Fig. 4.

FIG. 6. Results of tripartite steerings versus gac2 and θ: Sc1→mb (a), Smb→c1 (b), Sc2→mb (d) and Smb→c2 (e). The results involving c1 and c2 are summarized in (c) and (f), re- spectively. The blank area represents the unstable regime. All parameters remain the same as those in Fig. 4.
FIG. 8. The collective pentapartite steering Sc ac1c2b→m versus gac2 and θ. The blank area represents the unstable regime. All parameters remain the same as those in Fig. 4.
